name: Monstera
tagline: The world's most iconic houseplant. Almost impossible to kill and grows to jaw-dropping size.

water.detail: Water when top 2–5cm of soil is dry. Overwatering is the main mistake.

sun.detail: Thrives in bright filtered light. Direct sun scorches leaves.

soil.detail: Chunky potting mix with perlite. Roots need oxygen — don't compact.

temp.detail: No cold draughts. Below 10°C causes damage.

seedDepth.detail: Take a cutting with at least one node, root in water or moist compost.

tips: Those holes in the leaves are normal — they appear as the plant matures and indicate good health.
Give it a moss pole — it grows significantly bigger leaves with something to climb.
Wipe leaves monthly with a damp cloth — large leaves collect dust fast.
Yellow leaves usually mean overwatering. Let soil dry out more between waterings.
warning: All parts are toxic to cats, dogs and humans if ingested. Keep away from pets and children.

name: Snake Plant
tagline: The world's most searched houseplant. Survives neglect, low light, and irregular watering with ease.

water.detail: Let soil dry completely between waterings. In winter, once every 6–8 weeks.

sun.detail: Survives very low light. Better growth in brighter spots — but it really does handle anything.

soil.detail: Cactus mix or regular mix with added grit. Must drain freely.

temp.detail: Tolerates a wide range. Avoid below 10°C.

seedDepth.detail: Cut a leaf into sections and push into gritty compost, or divide at the root.

tips: The only reliable way to kill it is overwatering — root rot is fast and usually fatal.
Wipe leaves with a damp cloth occasionally to keep them glossy.
Produces oxygen at night — a popular choice for bedrooms.
Repot only every 2–3 years — it genuinely prefers being root-bound.
warning: Mildly toxic to cats and dogs if eaten. Can cause nausea and vomiting.

name: Pothos
tagline: Practically grows itself. Trails beautifully, tolerates neglect, and propagates endlessly for free.

water.detail: Water when top 2–3cm is dry. Yellow leaves usually mean overwatering.

sun.detail: Incredibly adaptable. Variegated varieties need more light to keep their pattern.

soil.detail: Standard potting mix with some perlite.

temp.detail: No cold draughts. Below 10°C causes leaf drop.

seedDepth.detail: Cut just below a node, place in water. Roots in 2–4 weeks then pot up.

tips: Propagate endlessly — put cuttings in a glass of water on the windowsill and pot when rooted.
Pale, washed-out leaves on a variegated variety means not enough light.
A single plant can trail several metres — great for high shelves.
Pinch growing tips to make it bushier instead of trailing.
warning: Toxic to cats and dogs. Can cause oral irritation and vomiting if eaten.

name: Aloe Vera
tagline: The most forgiving succulent you can own. Neglect it and it thrives. Overwater it and it dies.

water.detail: Water deeply then let soil dry completely. In winter, once a month.

sun.detail: Loves bright light. Some direct sun is fine but intense midday sun scorches tips.

soil.detail: Drainage is everything. Add perlite or grit if using regular potting mix.

temp.detail: Bring indoors if temperatures drop below 5°C.

seedDepth.detail: Separate baby plants that appear at the base and pot them individually.

tips: Thin curling leaves = thirsty. Soft mushy base = overwatered — the two key signs to watch.
Always use a pot with drainage holes. Standing water causes root rot.
Repot only when roots emerge from the bottom — aloe likes being root-bound.
The clear gel inside is genuinely soothing — slice a lower leaf for minor burns or dry skin.
warning: Root rot from overwatering is almost always fatal and irreversible. When in doubt, don't water.

name: Peace Lily
tagline: The best houseplant for dark rooms. Droops dramatically when thirsty then recovers within the hour.

water.detail: Water when top 2–3cm is dry. It droops visibly when thirsty — that's your reminder.

sun.detail: Survives quite dark rooms. Direct sun scorches the leaves brown.

soil.detail: Standard houseplant mix. Repot every 1–2 years.

temp.detail: Hates cold draughts and temperatures below 12°C.

seedDepth.detail: Not grown from seed at home. Divide the root ball when repotting.

care.harvest: Flowers in spring, sometimes twice yearly

tips: Brown leaf tips = air too dry. Put the pot on a tray of damp pebbles.
Wipe leaves with a damp cloth monthly to remove dust.
Don't rush to repot — peace lilies flower better when slightly root-bound.
NASA-verified air purifier — removes formaldehyde and other VOCs.
warning: Toxic to cats, dogs, and humans if ingested. Causes burning and swelling of mouth. Wash hands after handling sap.

name: Spider Plant
tagline: The ultimate beginner houseplant. Produces baby plants constantly and is practically impossible to kill.

water.detail: Water when soil is partly dry. Very forgiving of irregular watering.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light is ideal. Tolerates lower light very well.

soil.detail: Standard houseplant mix. Not fussy at all.

temp.detail: Very adaptable. Tolerates cool rooms but not frost.

seedDepth.detail: Pin the baby plants (spiderettes) onto moist compost while still attached to the mother plant.

tips: Pin baby plants (spiderettes) onto compost in a separate pot while still attached — snip the runner once rooted.
Brown leaf tips are caused by fluoride in tap water — use filtered or rainwater.
One pot produces dozens of baby plants in a season — perfect for gifts.
Looks spectacular in hanging baskets where the long trailing runners can dangle freely.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets — one of the very few completely safe houseplants for animal-owning households.

name: Rubber Plant
tagline: A classic houseplant that never goes out of style. Big glossy leaves, architectural shape, very tough.

water.detail: Water when top 3–4cm of soil is dry. Reduce in winter.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light is best. Tolerates lower light but growth slows. Direct sun scorches.

soil.detail: Good quality potting compost with perlite for drainage.

temp.detail: No cold draughts or temperatures below 12°C.

seedDepth.detail: Take a cutting below a leaf node, allow the white sap to dry, then root in moist compost.

tips: Wipe the large glossy leaves with a damp cloth monthly — they collect dust which blocks light.
Rotate the pot a quarter turn each month for even growth — it leans toward light.
The white sap is a skin irritant — wear gloves when taking cuttings.
Variegated varieties (tricolor, tineke) need more light to keep their patterns vibrant.
warning: Toxic to cats and dogs. The white milky sap can irritate skin and cause issues if ingested.

name: Fiddle Leaf Fig
tagline: The most dramatic-looking houseplant you can own. Also the most demanding. Worth the effort if you get it right.

water.detail: Water thoroughly, let top 3–5cm dry out between waterings. Very sensitive to both over and underwatering.

sun.detail: Needs consistent bright light. A few hours of gentle morning sun is ideal.

soil.detail: Good quality potting mix with perlite. Must drain freely.

temp.detail: Hates temperature fluctuations. Keep away from AC vents, draughty windows, and exterior doors.

seedDepth.detail: Take a large cutting with 2–3 leaves, dip in rooting hormone, keep warm and humid.

tips: Once you find a spot it's happy in — don't move it. It drops leaves every time it's relocated.
Wipe each large leaf with a damp cloth monthly — dust blocks the light it needs.
Brown spots with yellow halo = overwatering. Brown crispy edges = underwatering or low humidity.
Feed monthly in spring and summer with a balanced liquid fertiliser.
warning: Drops leaves dramatically when stressed by moving, cold draughts, or sudden changes in watering. Patience and consistency are essential.

name: ZZ Plant
tagline: Thrives on neglect. Dark, dry rooms that kill everything else — the ZZ plant does fine.

water.detail: Stores water in thick roots. Let soil dry completely between waterings.

sun.detail: One of the most shade-tolerant houseplants. Direct sun bleaches the leaves.

soil.detail: Cactus mix or standard mix with added grit.

temp.detail: Tolerates a wide range. Avoid below 8°C.

seedDepth.detail: Very slow to propagate — patience required. Division is faster.

tips: Water even less in winter — once a month is often enough.
The thick underground rhizomes store water — it's genuinely drought-adapted.
Wipe the glossy leaves monthly for best appearance.
Perfect for offices and rooms with only artificial light.
warning: All parts are toxic if ingested and the sap can irritate skin. Wear gloves when handling and keep away from children and pets.

name: Calathea
tagline: The most beautiful patterned leaves in the houseplant world. Folds its leaves up at night like hands in prayer.

water.detail: Keep soil consistently moist but never waterlogged. Use room-temperature water.

sun.detail: Thrives in indirect light. Direct sun fades the intricate leaf patterns.

soil.detail: Peat-free multipurpose compost with some perlite.

temp.detail: Hates cold draughts, cold windows in winter, and temperatures below 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: Divide the root ball when repotting in spring.

tips: Use filtered or rainwater — calatheas are sensitive to the fluoride and chlorine in tap water, causing brown leaf edges.
Mist the leaves or use a pebble tray with water to increase humidity — they're tropical plants.
Brown edges are usually caused by low humidity or tap water. Crispy edges = too dry. Yellow leaves = overwatering.
The leaves fold up at night (nyctinasty) — this is completely normal and a sign of a healthy plant.
warning: Sensitive to tap water chemicals. Use rainwater or filtered water consistently for best results.

name: Heartleaf Philodendron
tagline: The easiest trailing houseplant after pothos. Fast-growing, tolerant, and endlessly propagatable.

water.detail: Water when top 2–3cm of soil is dry. Tolerates some drought.

sun.detail: Tolerates low light well. Brighter light produces faster growth and larger leaves.

soil.detail: Standard potting mix with perlite.

temp.detail: No cold draughts. Below 12°C causes damage.

seedDepth.detail: Cut below a node and root in water — incredibly easy and fast.

tips: Roots in water incredibly easily — one of the best plants for propagation beginners.
Yellow leaves almost always mean overwatering.
Pinch growing tips to encourage a bushier, fuller plant.
Naturally climbs — give it a pole to train upward for larger, more impressive leaves.
warning: Toxic to cats, dogs, and humans if ingested. Contains calcium oxalate crystals which cause mouth irritation.

name: Split-Leaf Philodendron
tagline: A dramatic floor plant with deeply cut tropical leaves. Makes any room feel like a jungle.

water.detail: Water when top 3–5cm of soil is dry. Reduce in winter.

sun.detail: Needs bright indirect light for the dramatic leaf shape. Low light = smaller, less-cut leaves.

soil.detail: Chunky potting mix with perlite or bark.

temp.detail: Tropical plant — no cold draughts or temperatures below 13°C.

seedDepth.detail: Take stem cuttings with a node, root in moist compost.

tips: Wipe the large dramatic leaves with a damp cloth to keep them dust-free and glossy.
Stake the central stem as it grows — it can become top-heavy.
Yellow leaves = overwatering. Brown tips = low humidity or underwatering.
Repot every 2 years into a pot 5cm larger — it grows into an impressive specimen plant.
warning: Toxic to cats, dogs, and humans if ingested. All parts contain calcium oxalate crystals.

name: Chinese Evergreen
tagline: One of the toughest houseplants. Survives low light, dry air, and irregular watering with beautiful patterned leaves.

water.detail: Water when top 3–4cm is dry. Tolerates drought much better than overwatering.

sun.detail: Dark green varieties tolerate low light. Colourful pink/red varieties need brighter indirect light.

soil.detail: Standard potting mix with extra perlite or sand.

temp.detail: Hates cold. Keep away from cold windows and below 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: Root stem cuttings in water or divide the plant when repotting.

tips: One of NASA's top air-purifying plants.
Brighter-coloured varieties (pink, red, orange) need more light than dark green ones.
Wipe leaves regularly — they accumulate dust quickly.
Feed monthly in spring and summer with a balanced liquid fertiliser.
warning: Toxic to cats and dogs. Contains calcium oxalate crystals — causes mouth and stomach irritation.

name: Dracaena
tagline: Architectural and striking. Long slender leaves on bare stems give it a dramatic palm-like silhouette.

water.detail: Let soil dry out between waterings. Very drought-tolerant — one of the most forgiving houseplants.

sun.detail: Prefers bright indirect light but tolerates low light. Direct sun scorches the leaf tips.

soil.detail: Good quality potting compost — not too rich.

temp.detail: No cold draughts. Below 13°C causes leaf drop.

seedDepth.detail: Cut a stem section and push into moist compost. Root in a warm bright spot.

tips: Brown leaf tips are almost always caused by fluoride in tap water — switch to filtered or rainwater.
Trim brown tips with scissors (cut at an angle to mimic the natural leaf shape).
Slow-growing but very long-lived — a well-cared-for dracaena can live for decades.
Multiple stems can be planted together in one pot for a fuller, more dramatic look.
warning: Toxic to cats and dogs. Can cause vomiting and dilated pupils in cats.

name: Bird of Paradise
tagline: The most dramatic statement houseplant you can own. Paddle-shaped leaves and stunning orange flowers indoors.

water.detail: Water when top 3–5cm is dry. Drought-tolerant once established.

sun.detail: Needs as much light as possible to thrive and eventually flower. South-facing window is ideal.

soil.detail: Good potting compost with perlite. Feed regularly during growing season.

temp.detail: Tolerates brief cold but prefers consistently warm conditions.

seedDepth.detail: Seeds take 3–5 years to flower. Division is much faster — split the root clump when repotting.

tips: Needs to be slightly root-bound to flower — don't rush to repot.
Split leaves are normal — caused by wind or low humidity. Doesn't damage the plant.
Feed every 2 weeks in spring and summer with a high-potassium fertiliser.
Move outdoors in summer in a sheltered sunny spot — it accelerates growth and can trigger flowering.
warning: Mildly toxic to cats and dogs if eaten. The flowers and seeds cause digestive upset.

name: Moth Orchid
tagline: The most popular flowering houseplant. Flowers last months and it blooms again every year.

water.detail: Water thoroughly then let dry out almost completely. Never let roots sit in water.

sun.detail: East or west-facing window ideal. Direct sun burns the leaves and fades flowers.

soil.value: Orchid bark or chunky bark mix
soil.detail: Never use regular compost — roots need air. Specialist orchid bark is essential.

temp.detail: Needs a cool night (3–5°C cooler than daytime) to trigger re-blooming.

seedDepth.detail: Baby plants (keikis) sometimes appear on the flower spike — pot these up when they have roots.

tips: After flowers drop, cut the spike just above the second node — a new spike often emerges from there.
A cool windowsill in autumn (15°C nights) triggers re-blooming — the temperature drop is the cue.
Transparent pots let you see the root condition — healthy roots are green-silver. Mushy grey roots = rot.
Ice cube watering is a myth — use room-temperature water and let it drain completely.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets. One of the few completely safe flowering houseplants.

name: Anthurium
tagline: One of the longest-flowering houseplants. Those glossy red 'flowers' can last up to 3 months.

water.detail: Water when top 2–3cm is dry. Needs consistent moisture but no waterlogging.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light produces the most flowers. Direct sun bleaches the spathes.

soil.detail: Orchid bark mixed with potting compost. Roots need excellent drainage and air.

temp.detail: Tropical plant — no cold draughts or below 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: Divide when the plant has multiple stems, or take stem cuttings with a node.

tips: The 'flowers' are actually modified leaves (spathes) — the true flowers are the tiny ones on the central spike.
Feed every 2 weeks with a high-phosphorus fertiliser during the growing season to encourage more blooms.
Mist occasionally or use a pebble tray — they love humidity.
Wipe the glossy leaves with a damp cloth to keep them shiny.
warning: Toxic to cats, dogs, and humans. Causes burning and swelling of the mouth and throat if ingested.

name: Jade Plant
tagline: The money plant. A tough, slow-growing succulent that can live for 100+ years with minimal care.

water.detail: Let soil dry out completely between waterings. In winter, reduce to once a month.

sun.detail: Loves direct sun. South or east-facing window is ideal. Insufficient light = leggy, weak growth.

soil.detail: Gritty, fast-draining soil is essential. Regular compost holds too much moisture.

temp.detail: Tolerates cool temperatures but not frost. Bring indoors below 5°C.

seedDepth.detail: Let cuttings dry for 2–3 days before potting into dry compost. Water only after 1 week.

tips: Let cuttings callous (dry) for 2–3 days before planting — prevents rotting at the cut end.
Red leaf edges in bright sun are normal and actually desirable — a sign of a happy plant.
Repot in spring every 2–3 years — jade grows slowly but steadily.
Can become bonsai-like with age — some specimens live for decades and become impressive tree-like shapes.
warning: Mildly toxic to cats and dogs. Can cause vomiting and lethargy if eaten.

name: Echeveria
tagline: The perfect windowsill succulent. Rosette-shaped, colourful, and almost thrives on neglect.

water.detail: Water thoroughly then let dry completely. 'Soak and dry' method is best.

sun.detail: Needs direct sunlight to maintain compact shape and vibrant colour. Stretched, pale growth = not enough light.

soil.detail: Fast-draining mix is essential. Add grit or perlite to regular compost.

temp.detail: Tolerates brief cold but not frost. Hardy down to about 5°C.

seedDepth.detail: Twist a healthy leaf off cleanly, let dry for 2 days, then lay on moist gritty compost.

tips: 'Soak and dry' method: water thoroughly until it drains, then don't water again until completely dry.
Etiolation (stretched, pale growth) means not enough light — move to a brighter spot immediately.
Bright sun stress colours the tips red, orange, or purple — this is healthy and very pretty.
Remove dead lower leaves to prevent pest harbourage and rot.
warning: Overwatering and insufficient light are the two main killers. Both are irreversible once root rot sets in.

name: Desert Cactus
tagline: The ultimate low-maintenance plant. Survives weeks without water and produces surprising blooms.

water.detail: Water when completely dry in summer. In winter, barely water at all — once every 6–8 weeks.

sun.detail: Needs as much direct sun as possible. A south-facing windowsill is ideal.

soil.detail: 50% cactus compost, 50% grit or perlite. Drainage is critical.

temp.detail: Most desert cacti need a cool dry winter (5–12°C) to trigger spring blooms.

seedDepth.detail: Press seeds onto surface of cactus mix, barely cover. Most cacti produce small offset 'pups' that can be separated.

care.harvest: Flowers in spring after cool dry winter

tips: A cool, dry winter rest (minimal watering, cooler temperature) triggers the best spring flowering.
Never let the pot sit in a saucer of water — even briefly.
Terracotta pots are ideal — they breathe and dry out faster than plastic.
Spring flowers are often surprisingly large and vivid for such small plants — worth the wait.
warning: The spines are obviously painful. Wear thick gloves or use folded newspaper to handle. Some species have hair-like glochids that are more irritating than the spines.

name: Christmas Cactus
tagline: Flowers prolifically in winter when almost nothing else does. A reliable, long-lived houseplant gift.

water.detail: More regular watering than desert cacti. Keep moist during flowering. Reduce after blooming.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light. Direct sun can cause bleaching.

soil.detail: Regular potting mix with added perlite. Slightly acidic pH preferred.

temp.detail: Needs short days and cool temperatures (below 15°C at night) in autumn to trigger flowering.

seedDepth.detail: Twist off 2–3 leaf segments, let callous for a day, then push into moist compost.

tips: To trigger flowering: from October, give 14 hours of darkness per day and keep at 15°C for 6 weeks.
During the rest period (spring–summer), water less and stop feeding.
Feed monthly from spring to early autumn with a balanced fertiliser.
Can live for 20–30 years — a true heirloom plant that gets more spectacular with age.
warning: Mildly toxic to cats and dogs. Can cause vomiting and diarrhoea if eaten in quantity.

name: Hoya (Wax Plant)
tagline: Waxy, fragrant star-shaped flowers appear year after year. Tolerates neglect and propagates easily.

water.detail: Let soil dry out between waterings. Tolerates drought but not waterlogging.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light is ideal. Some gentle direct morning sun is fine.

soil.detail: Mix of potting compost and orchid bark or perlite. Excellent drainage essential.

temp.detail: No cold below 10°C. Keep away from draughts.

seedDepth.detail: Take a 10–15cm cutting with 2–3 leaf pairs. Root in water or moist compost.

care.harvest: Fragrant flowers appear on spurs — mainly in summer
care.spacing: Trails or climbs — can reach several metres

tips: Never remove old flower spurs (the small knobby stems) — new flowers grow from the same spots each year.
A period of cooler, drier conditions in winter can trigger flowering in spring.
There are hundreds of hoya species and varieties — all have slightly different needs but the same basics.
Wonderfully long-lived — some hoyas are passed down through generations.
warning: The milky sap can irritate skin in sensitive individuals. Not highly toxic but best kept away from pets.

name: Bromeliad
tagline: A striking tropical plant with a central cup that holds water. The coloured bract lasts months.

water.detail: Fill the central rosette cup with water. Flush and refill monthly to prevent stagnation. Keep soil lightly moist.

sun.detail: Bright filtered light. Direct sun bleaches the colour.

soil.detail: Specialist bromeliad mix or regular compost with lots of perlite.

temp.detail: Tropical plant — no cold draughts or below 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: The main plant flowers once then dies. Baby pups grow at the base — pot these up when 15cm tall.

tips: Water in the central cup, not the soil. Change the cup water monthly to prevent mosquito larvae.
After flowering, the main plant slowly dies — this is normal. The pups take over.
Pot up pups when they're about 1/3 the size of the mother plant with their own roots.
Ethylene gas (from a ripe apple placed near the plant in a bag) can trigger flowering in reluctant bromeliads.
warning: The central cup can harbour stagnant water — change it monthly. Non-toxic to humans and pets.

name: Air Plant
tagline: No soil needed. They absorb water from the air. Display them anywhere — driftwood, shells, glass globes.

water.value: Mist 2–3 times per week or soak weekly
water.detail: Mist thoroughly 2–3 times a week or soak in room-temperature water for 20–30 minutes once a week. Shake off excess water after soaking.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light is ideal. Direct sun dries them out too fast.

soil.detail: Mount on driftwood, cork bark, or display in glass globes. Never bury in soil.

temp.detail: Warm temperatures. No frost.

seedDepth.detail: Pups appear after flowering. Leave attached until 1/3 the mother's size, then separate.

tips: After soaking, always shake gently and let dry within 4 hours — water trapped in the base causes rot.
Use rainwater or filtered water — chlorine and fluoride damage them over time.
They flower once in their lifetime, then produce 2–8 pups before dying.
Silver-leafed varieties are more drought-tolerant. Green-leafed varieties need more frequent watering.
warning: The number one killer is rot from trapped water. Always allow to dry upside-down after soaking or misting.

name: Money Tree
tagline: The braided trunk makes it a striking feature plant. Easy to grow and associated with good luck in many cultures.

water.detail: Water thoroughly then let top 3–5cm dry out. Reduce in winter.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light is ideal. Tolerates some lower light. Direct sun scorches the leaves.

soil.detail: Good quality potting compost with perlite.

temp.detail: Consistent temperatures. Hates cold draughts.

seedDepth.detail: Seeds germinate easily. Stem cuttings also work.

tips: The braided trunk is made from multiple seedlings woven together when young — the braid is permanent.
Rotate the pot occasionally for even growth toward the light.
Yellowing leaves usually mean overwatering or a draught.
A popular Feng Shui plant — traditionally placed in the 'wealth corner' (south-east) of a room.
warning: Generally considered non-toxic but keep away from pets as a precaution — some animals show sensitivity.

name: Boston Fern
tagline: Lush, feathery fronds that trail beautifully. Loves humidity — perfect for bathrooms.

water.detail: Keep soil consistently moist. Never let it dry out completely — fronds brown and drop quickly.

sun.detail: Prefers indirect light. Tolerates quite low light. Direct sun scorches the delicate fronds.

soil.detail: Peat-free compost with good moisture retention. Mist around the pot to increase humidity.

temp.detail: Prefers cool to moderate temperatures. Hates dry, hot indoor air.

seedDepth.detail: Divide the root ball in spring or pot up runners (stolons) that appear at the base.

tips: Humidity is the key — bathrooms or kitchens are ideal. Mist daily or use a pebble tray.
Yellow, dropping fronds are normal in winter as light levels drop — not a sign of failure.
Repot every year in spring — boston ferns are vigorous and quickly become root-bound.
Hang in a basket for the most spectacular trailing effect.
warning: Non-toxic to cats, dogs, and humans. One of the safest lush houseplants for pet owners.

name: Bird's Nest Fern
tagline: Wavy bright green fronds growing from a central rosette. More forgiving than most ferns.

water.detail: Keep moist but not waterlogged. Water around the edges — never into the central rosette.

sun.detail: Tolerates low light well. One of the best ferns for shadier rooms.

soil.detail: Good quality peat-free compost with some bark for drainage.

temp.detail: Tropical fern — hates cold draughts and below 13°C.

seedDepth.detail: Growing from spores takes years. Buy a small plant instead.

tips: Never water directly into the central cup — this causes crown rot. Water around the edges only.
The wavy leaf edges ('Crispy Wave' variety) are extremely air-purifying according to research.
Fronds emerge tightly furled from the centre — be patient as they unfurl slowly.
Brown marks on fronds from touching are permanent — handle carefully.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets. Safe household choice.

name: Maidenhair Fern
tagline: The most delicate and beautiful of all ferns. Also the most demanding — rewards patience richly.

water.detail: Never let it dry out. Even one missed watering can cause total frond drop. Consistent moisture is essential.

sun.detail: Bright but indirect light. Direct sun or strong light will scorch the delicate fronds.

soil.detail: Peat-free compost with good moisture retention. A slightly larger pot helps with moisture consistency.

temp.detail: Consistent temperature. Hates cold draughts and hot dry air.

seedDepth.detail: Divide carefully in spring — the root system is delicate.

tips: If it dries out and all fronds die — don't throw it away. Cut back dead fronds and keep consistently moist. New growth often appears.
A bathroom is the ideal location — the humidity from showers keeps it happy.
Never put near a radiator, air vent, or draughty window.
Mist daily or use a pebble tray — humidity is as important as watering.
warning: Will die (or appear to die) at the first sign of neglect. Not for beginners or anyone who travels frequently.

name: Lucky Bamboo
tagline: Not actually bamboo — it's a dracaena. Grows in just water, symbolises good luck, and lasts for years.

water.detail: Grow in 5–8cm of water. Change the water completely every 1–2 weeks.

sun.detail: Tolerates very low light. Direct sun causes yellowing.

soil.detail: Can grow in water only with some pebbles to support the stems, or in well-draining compost.

temp.detail: Warm temperatures. No cold below 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: Cut a stem with a node and place in water. Roots emerge in 2–4 weeks.

tips: Use filtered or bottled water — chlorine in tap water causes yellow leaves. Let tap water sit overnight before using.
The spiral and braided shapes are achieved by rotating toward light slowly over months.
Yellow leaves are usually caused by too much direct light, chlorine in water, or overfertilising.
Fertilise very sparingly — one drop of liquid fertiliser per month in the water is enough.
warning: Toxic to cats and dogs. Despite the feng shui popularity, keep away from pets.

name: Umbrella Plant
tagline: Fast-growing with dramatic umbrella-shaped leaf clusters. Tolerates some neglect and shapes well.

water.detail: Water when top 3–4cm of soil is dry. Drought-tolerant but consistent watering is best.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light produces the best growth. Tolerates some lower light.

soil.detail: Good quality potting compost with perlite.

temp.detail: No cold draughts or below 12°C.

seedDepth.detail: Take tip cuttings and root in moist compost with some warmth.

care.spacing: 60–90 cm — can become a small tree

tips: Prune back aggressively if it gets too leggy — it recovers quickly and grows back bushier.
Wipe leaves regularly — the many small leaves collect dust.
Variegated varieties need more light to keep their colouring.
Can be trained into a bonsai-like form over many years with regular pruning.
warning: Toxic to cats and dogs. Can cause vomiting and mouth irritation.

name: String of Pearls
tagline: One of the most visually striking trailing succulents. Long strings of perfect spherical leaves.

water.detail: Water when soil is dry. Pearls will appear slightly shrivelled when thirsty — that's your cue.

sun.detail: Needs bright light with some direct sun. Insufficient light causes spacing between pearls.

soil.detail: Very fast-draining mix. 50% cactus compost, 50% grit or perlite.

temp.detail: No cold or frost. Below 10°C causes damage.

seedDepth.detail: Lay a strand on moist cactus compost and pin down. Roots form at the nodes.

tips: Shrivelled pearls = thirsty. Mushy pearls = overwatered.
Space between pearls means not enough light — move to a brighter spot.
A hanging pot shows off the trailing strands best.
Propagate easily — lay a strand on moist compost, pin it down, and new roots form at each node.
warning: Toxic to cats, dogs, and humans. Can cause vomiting and skin irritation.

name: String of Hearts
tagline: Delicate heart-shaped leaves on long trailing vines. One of the most charming small trailing plants.

water.detail: Let soil dry out almost completely between waterings. Very drought-tolerant.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light produces the best leaf variegation. Too much direct sun can bleach the leaves.

soil.detail: Cactus mix or potting compost with added perlite.

temp.detail: No cold. Below 10°C causes damage.

seedDepth.detail: Small tubers form along the stems — pot these directly into compost. Or coil stems on moist compost.

tips: The small tubers along the stems can be planted directly into compost — instant propagation.
Pale, washed-out leaves mean not enough light.
Goes semi-dormant in winter — water much less.
The trailing vines look beautiful draped over a shelf or in a hanging pot.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets. Safe and charming choice for any household.

name: Croton
tagline: The most colourful foliage houseplant. Vivid red, orange, yellow, and green leaves — indoors all year.

water.detail: Keep soil consistently moist during growing season. Reduce slightly in winter.

sun.detail: Needs plenty of light to maintain vivid colours. Dull light = all-green leaves.

soil.detail: Good quality potting compost with perlite.

temp.detail: Hates cold, draughts, and temperature fluctuations — causes leaf drop.

seedDepth.detail: Take 8–10cm tip cuttings, dip in rooting hormone, root in moist compost with warmth.

tips: The brighter the light, the more vivid the colours — a sunny window produces the most dramatic look.
Leaf drop when you bring it home is normal — it's adjusting. Keep conditions stable and it will recover.
Mist occasionally or use a pebble tray — it enjoys humidity.
The sap is an irritant — wear gloves when taking cuttings.
warning: Toxic to cats, dogs, and humans. The sap causes skin irritation and gastrointestinal upset if ingested.

name: Polka Dot Plant
tagline: Bright pink, red, or white spots on green leaves. A compact, cheerful little plant for bright spots.

water.detail: Keep consistently moist. Wilts quickly when thirsty but recovers fast.

sun.detail: Needs good light to maintain the vivid spot colours. Low light fades the pattern.

soil.detail: Good quality potting compost.

temp.detail: Warm temperatures. No cold or draughts.

seedDepth.detail: Easy from seed. Germinates quickly and grows fast.

tips: Pinch out the growing tips regularly to keep it bushy — it gets leggy quickly otherwise.
Pinch off flower spikes as they appear — after flowering the plant tends to die. Pinching extends its life.
Great for terrariums — loves humidity and compact conditions.
Easy to start fresh from seed each year if the plant gets old and tired.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets. Safe choice for households with animals and children.

name: Caladium
tagline: Heart-shaped leaves in spectacular pink, red, and white patterns. Dramatic seasonal colour.

water.detail: Keep moist during growing season. Goes dormant in winter — stop watering as leaves die back.

sun.detail: Bright but indirect. Direct sun scorches the delicate leaves. Pink varieties need more light than green ones.

soil.detail: Good quality compost with perlite. Needs good aeration.

temp.detail: Tropical plant — hates cold and draughts. Below 18°C causes dormancy.

seedDepth.detail: Plant tubers (knobby side up) 3–5cm deep in warm soil. Sprouts in 2–4 weeks.

tips: Goes dormant in winter — leaves die back naturally. Store dry tubers at 15°C and restart in spring.
Warm, humid conditions produce the biggest, most vibrant leaves.
Don't try to force it through winter — embrace the dormancy cycle.
Multiple tubers in one large pot creates a fuller, more impressive display.
warning: Toxic to cats, dogs, and humans. Contains calcium oxalate crystals — causes mouth burning and swelling.

name: Nerve Plant
tagline: Intricate white or pink veining on deep green leaves. Perfect for terrariums and humid environments.

water.detail: Keep consistently moist. Wilts dramatically when thirsty but recovers almost instantly when watered.

sun.detail: Prefers low light. Direct sun causes bleaching and leaf burn.

soil.detail: Peat-free compost with good moisture retention.

temp.detail: Tropical plant — hates cold, draughts, and dry air.

seedDepth.detail: Root stem cuttings easily in moist compost. Keep warm and humid.

tips: Perfect terrarium plant — loves the enclosed humid conditions.
The dramatic wilting when thirsty is a theatrical reminder, not a death sentence — it bounces back fast.
Pinch out growing tips to keep it compact and bushy.
Group with other humidity-loving plants to create a naturally humid microclimate.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets. Safe choice.

name: Prayer Plant
tagline: Folds its striking patterned leaves upright at night like hands in prayer. A conversation piece.

water.detail: Keep consistently moist but not waterlogged. Use room-temperature water.

sun.detail: Tolerates low light well. Direct sun fades the leaf patterns.

soil.detail: Good quality peat-free compost with perlite.

temp.detail: Hates cold draughts and below 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: Root stem cuttings in water or divide when repotting.

tips: Use filtered or rainwater — sensitive to chlorine and fluoride in tap water.
The night movement (nyctinasty) is completely normal — it's the plant responding to light changes.
Brown edges = low humidity or tap water chemicals. Mist regularly or use a pebble tray.
Goes semi-dormant in winter — water less frequently.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets. A safe, beautiful choice.

name: Wandering Dude
tagline: Vivid purple and silver striped leaves on fast-trailing stems. Impossible to kill and extremely easy to propagate.

water.detail: Water when top 2–3cm is dry. Very forgiving of irregular watering.

sun.detail: Brighter light = more vivid purple. Low light = the plant goes mostly green.

soil.detail: Standard potting compost. Not fussy.

temp.detail: Tolerates a wide range. No frost.

seedDepth.detail: Pop cuttings in water — roots appear in 1–2 weeks. One of the fastest-rooting houseplants.

tips: Propagates incredibly easily — a single stem in water roots in days.
Pinch back regularly or it gets straggly. The pinched stems can all be propagated.
The vivid purple fades to green in low light — this is the most reliable light indicator.
Looks spectacular in a hanging basket or trailing from a high shelf.
warning: The sap can cause skin irritation in sensitive individuals. Mildly toxic to cats and dogs.

name: Chinese Money Plant
tagline: The trendiest houseplant of the last decade. Round coin-shaped leaves on long stems. Very easy to grow.

water.detail: Water when top 3–4cm is dry. Droopy leaves are the sign it needs water.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light is ideal. Too much direct sun scorches. Too little = leggy stems.

soil.detail: Standard potting mix with perlite.

temp.detail: Tolerates slightly cooler temperatures than most tropical houseplants.

seedDepth.detail: Baby plants (pups) grow at the base and can be separated when a few centimetres tall.

tips: Rotate the pot a quarter turn every week — it leans dramatically toward light.
Baby plants (pups) grow at the base — pot them up and share with friends (hence its other name: the friendship plant).
Yellow leaves usually mean overwatering. Curling leaves = underwatering.
Feed monthly in spring and summer with a balanced liquid fertiliser.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets. A completely safe choice.

name: Peperomia
tagline: Hundreds of varieties, all easy. Compact, low-maintenance, and great for small spaces and shelves.

water.detail: Let soil dry out between waterings. Stores water in its thick leaves — drought-tolerant.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light suits most varieties. Patterned varieties need more light to keep markings.

soil.detail: Standard potting mix with extra perlite. Good drainage is key.

temp.detail: No cold or draughts. Below 10°C causes damage.

seedDepth.detail: Most varieties root easily from leaf cuttings pushed into moist compost.

tips: There are over 1,000 species — watermelon peperomia, ripple peperomia, and rubber peperomia are the most popular.
The thick succulent-like leaves store water — much more drought-tolerant than most houseplants.
Great for terrariums — compact size and humidity tolerance suit enclosed environments.
Leaf cuttings work well — insert a leaf petiole (stem) into moist compost for new plants.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets. One of the safest houseplants.

name: Arrowhead Plant
tagline: Arrow-shaped leaves that change shape as the plant matures. Trails, climbs, or bushes depending on how you train it.

water.detail: Water when top 2–3cm is dry. Tolerates some drought.

sun.detail: Very adaptable. Variegated varieties need more light to keep their colouring.

soil.detail: Standard potting compost with perlite.

temp.detail: No cold draughts or below 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: Root easily in water — one of the easiest houseplants to propagate.

tips: Young plants have arrow-shaped leaves. Mature plants develop lobed, multi-fingered leaves — very different.
Pinch growing tips for a bushy shape. Let it climb a pole for larger, more dramatic mature leaves.
Pink and variegated varieties need brighter light to keep their colouring.
Propagates incredibly easily in water — great for beginners.
warning: Toxic to cats, dogs, and humans. Causes mouth and stomach irritation if eaten.

name: Dumb Cane
tagline: Large, boldly patterned tropical leaves. Fast-growing and very tolerant of indoor conditions.

water.detail: Water when top 3–4cm is dry. Tolerates some drought but prefers consistent moisture.

sun.detail: Tolerates low light well. Brighter light produces more vivid leaf markings.

soil.detail: Good quality potting compost with perlite.

temp.detail: No cold below 15°C. Hates draughts.

seedDepth.detail: Cut stem sections with nodes and root in moist compost.

tips: One of the most tolerant large-leaved houseplants for low-light rooms.
Wipe the large patterned leaves with a damp cloth to keep them looking their best.
Can be cut back hard if it gets too leggy — new growth appears quickly from the base.
Feed monthly in spring and summer for the best leaf size and colour.
warning: Highly toxic — all parts contain calcium oxalate crystals that cause temporary inability to speak (hence 'dumb cane'). Can cause severe mouth burning, swelling, and throat irritation. Keep away from children and pets.

name: Cast Iron Plant
tagline: Earns its name completely. Survives dust, draughts, deep shade, and total neglect.

water.detail: Water when soil is dry. Very drought-tolerant.

sun.detail: One of the few plants that actually thrives in deep shade. Direct sun scorches.

soil.detail: Genuinely not fussy. Almost any compost works.

temp.detail: Remarkable cold tolerance. Hardy down to about 5°C indoors.

seedDepth.detail: Divide the rhizome when repotting in spring. Each section with roots and a shoot will grow.

tips: The ideal plant for dark hallways, stairwells, and rooms with no natural light.
Grows very slowly — don't expect rapid results.
Wipe the long leathery leaves occasionally — they do collect dust.
Virtually indestructible — a good choice for people who have killed every other houseplant.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets. Genuinely one of the safest, toughest choices you can make.

name: Golden Pothos
tagline: The classic golden-variegated trailing plant. Unkillable, fast-growing, and propagates endlessly.

water.detail: Let top 3cm dry out between waterings.

sun.detail: Tolerates low light. The golden variegation is best maintained in brighter conditions.

soil.detail: Not fussy. Any decent potting compost works.

temp.detail: Very tolerant range.

seedDepth.detail: Root in water in 1–2 weeks.

tips: The gold markings fade to green in low light — the plant's most reliable light indicator.
One of the best plants for absorbing indoor air pollutants.
Propagates so easily it's often given as a first houseplant.
Pinch tips to create a bushier plant instead of a long trailer.
warning: Toxic to cats and dogs. Contains calcium oxalate crystals.

name: Parlour Palm
tagline: The most forgiving indoor palm. Tolerates low light and irregular watering unlike most palms.

water.detail: Water when top 3cm is dry. Do not overwater — root rot is the main risk.

sun.detail: Tolerates low light. Avoid direct sun — it scorches the delicate fronds.

soil.detail: Good quality potting compost with perlite. Must drain well.

temp.detail: No cold draughts or below 12°C.

seedDepth.detail: Seeds take months to germinate and years to grow. Buy a plant.

tips: One of the best palms for dim indoor conditions — most palms fail indoors, this one thrives.
Wipe the feathery fronds occasionally to remove dust.
Brown leaf tips are common — caused by dry air or fluoride in water. Use rainwater.
Can produce small flowers and berries indoors in good conditions.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets. Safe choice.

name: Kentia Palm
tagline: The most elegant indoor palm. Slow-growing, incredibly tough, and a fixture in luxury hotels.

water.detail: Water when top 3–5cm is dry. Tolerates drought well once established.

sun.detail: Prefers bright indirect light but tolerates lower light conditions.

soil.detail: Good quality compost with perlite. Excellent drainage essential.

temp.detail: Tolerates slightly cooler temperatures than most palms.

seedDepth.detail: Very slow from seed. Buy a plant — they are long-lived and get more impressive with age.

tips: Slow-growing but extremely long-lived — a quality specimen is a decades-long companion.
Wipe the long fronds with a damp cloth to remove dust.
Feed sparingly — once a month in summer with half-strength fertiliser.
Does not like being repotted frequently — move up only one pot size when roots emerge from the bottom.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets. Safe and elegant.

name: Areca Palm
tagline: The best palm for humid homes. Feathery arching fronds and excellent air humidifying properties.

water.detail: Keep moist during summer. Reduce in winter. Never let roots sit in water.

sun.detail: Needs bright indirect light. Direct sun scorches. Low light causes yellowing.

soil.detail: Good quality compost with perlite. pH 6.0–6.5 is ideal.

temp.detail: No cold below 15°C. Hates draughts.

seedDepth.detail: Seeds germinate in 6 weeks but grow slowly. Buy a multi-stemmed plant for instant impact.

tips: One of the best natural humidifiers — releases significant moisture into the air.
Brown tips are common and cosmetic — caused by dry air or tap water. Trim and use filtered water.
Remove yellowing fronds from the base to keep the plant looking tidy.
Feed monthly in summer with a palm-specific fertiliser.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets. Safe choice.

name: Yucca
tagline: Architectural sword-like leaves on a stout trunk. Drought-tolerant and almost indestructible indoors.

water.detail: Let soil dry completely between waterings. Very drought-tolerant.

sun.detail: Loves light. A sunny windowsill is ideal. Tolerates lower light but grows more slowly.

soil.detail: Cactus mix or potting compost with lots of grit.

temp.detail: Tolerates a wide range including some cold.

seedDepth.detail: Trunk sections can be rooted. Offsets that appear at the base can be separated and potted.

tips: Wipe the sharp-edged leaves carefully — they are genuinely sharp.
In summer, move outside to a sunny sheltered spot for accelerated growth.
Water much less in winter — once a month is enough.
The lower leaves naturally yellow and drop as the trunk develops — this is normal.
warning: The leaf tips are sharp enough to puncture skin. Place away from high-traffic areas, especially with children.

name: Indoor Banana
tagline: Enormous tropical leaves that make any room feel like a jungle. Can fruit indoors in very good conditions.

water.detail: Thirsty plant. Keep consistently moist. Reduce in winter.

sun.detail: Needs as much light as possible — a south-facing window or conservatory.

soil.detail: Rich potting compost with good drainage. Feed generously during growing season.

temp.detail: Tropical plant — no cold, no draughts, no below 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: Separate offsets (pups) from the base when they have their own roots.

care.harvest: Fruit possible after 18+ months in ideal conditions

tips: Wipe the large leaves with a damp cloth to maximise light absorption.
Feed every 2 weeks with a high-nitrogen fertiliser during the growing season.
The main trunk flowers and fruits once then dies — pups continue the plant.
Move outside in summer to a warm sheltered spot for the best growth.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets. Safe tropical statement plant.

name: Elephant Ear
tagline: Spectacular dark green leaves with dramatic white veins. A striking architectural statement plant.

water.detail: Keep moist but never waterlogged. The main killer is root rot from overwatering.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light is essential. Direct sun scorches the dramatic leaves.

soil.detail: Chunky potting mix with perlite and bark. Roots need excellent aeration.

temp.detail: Tropical plant — no cold draughts or below 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: Divide the corms when repotting or pot up offset pups.

tips: Goes dormant in winter — leaves may die back. Don't discard — reduce watering and wait for spring growth.
High humidity is important — group with other plants or use a pebble tray.
Wipe the dramatic veined leaves with a damp cloth.
Spider mites love them in dry indoor air — check the undersides of leaves regularly.
warning: Highly toxic to cats, dogs, and humans. Causes severe mouth burning and irritation. Keep well away from children and pets.

name: Taro / Elephant Ear
tagline: Enormous, velvety heart-shaped leaves with a dramatic tropical presence.

water.detail: Loves moisture — water frequently and mist regularly.

sun.detail: Prefers bright indirect light. Tolerates some shade.

soil.detail: Rich compost with good moisture retention. Can even grow in water.

temp.detail: Tropical plant — no frost.

seedDepth.detail: Plant corms in spring. The knobbly corms sprout within a few weeks in warmth.

care.harvest: Tubers are edible when cooked. Grown primarily as an ornamental indoors.

tips: The tubers (corms) are edible when cooked — the plant is a staple crop in much of Asia and the Pacific.
Goes dormant in winter — die back is normal. Store corms dry at 10°C.
Varieties like 'Black Magic' (deep purple leaves) are dramatic and popular.
Can be grown in a pot of water — place the corm in shallow water with just the base submerged.
warning: All raw parts are toxic — contains calcium oxalate crystals. Must be cooked before eating. Causes severe burning if raw.

name: Stromanthe
tagline: Stunning three-tone leaves in pink, white, and green. Similar needs to calathea but slightly more forgiving.

water.detail: Keep moist but not waterlogged. Use room-temperature filtered water.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light maintains the vibrant pink colouring.

soil.detail: Peat-free compost with perlite.

temp.detail: No cold draughts or below 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: Divide at repotting time in spring.

tips: Use filtered or rainwater — sensitive to tap water chemicals.
The pink undersides of the leaves are revealed as they fold up at night.
More forgiving than calathea but similar care — treat it as an easier cousin.
High humidity helps — mist or use a pebble tray.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets.

name: Rex Begonia
tagline: The most spectacular foliage begonia. Leaves in metallic silver, red, purple, and green patterns.

water.detail: Water when top 2–3cm is dry. Sensitive to both overwatering and complete drought.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light produces the best leaf colours. Direct sun bleaches the patterns.

soil.detail: Peat-free compost with perlite. Good drainage essential.

temp.detail: No cold draughts or below 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: Cut a leaf into sections with veins, press onto moist compost. New plants emerge from the veins.

tips: Water from below (place pot in a saucer of water for 30 minutes) to avoid wet leaves which cause rot.
High humidity is important — but avoid misting directly on the leaves as this causes spotting.
Goes semi-dormant in winter — water less and stop feeding.
Leaf section propagation is fascinating and works remarkably well.
warning: All begonias are toxic to cats and dogs. Can cause vomiting and kidney failure in cats.

name: Cyclamen
tagline: Cheerful swept-back flowers in winter when colour is scarce. Grown as a winter flowering houseplant.

water.detail: Water from below by placing in a saucer of water. Never water into the centre crown — causes rot.

sun.detail: Cool, bright indirect light. Avoid hot sunny windowsills.

soil.detail: Standard potting compost. Good drainage essential.

temp.detail: Prefers cool conditions. A cool bright windowsill is ideal. Central heating makes it deteriorate faster.

seedDepth.detail: Grown from tubers. Seeds take 18 months to flower — buy a plant.

tips: Cool rooms extend the flowering period significantly. Avoid keeping on hot windowsills near radiators.
Remove dead flowers and yellow leaves by twisting and pulling — don't cut.
After flowering, reduce watering, allow to go dormant in summer, then restart in autumn.
Water from below — never into the crown of the tuber as this causes rot.
warning: Toxic to cats and dogs. Can cause digestive upset and, in large amounts, more serious symptoms.

name: Busy Lizzie
tagline: Non-stop cheerful flowers in vivid colours. One of the best plants for a bright windowsill.

water.detail: Keep consistently moist. Wilts fast when dry but recovers quickly when watered.

sun.detail: Bright light without direct harsh sun. East or west-facing window is ideal.

soil.detail: Good quality potting compost. Feed weekly during flowering.

temp.detail: Not frost-hardy. Bring indoors in autumn.

seedDepth.detail: Tiny seeds need light to germinate. Press onto moist compost. Or buy plug plants.

tips: Pinch out growing tips when young to encourage a bushy, flower-covered plant.
Feed weekly with a high-potassium liquid feed (tomato fertiliser) during flowering.
Deadheading is unnecessary — they are self-cleaning.
Take cuttings in autumn and overwinter indoors to have free plants next year.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets.

name: Oxalis (Wood Sorrel)
tagline: Deep purple clover-like leaves that open and close with the light. Charming, unique, and easy.

water.detail: Water when top soil is dry. Goes dormant in summer — reduce watering then.

sun.detail: Bright light produces the deepest purple colour. Low light = pale, sparse growth.

soil.detail: Standard potting compost with perlite.

temp.detail: Hardy to around 5°C. Tolerates cooler rooms.

seedDepth.detail: Plant small rhizomes just below the soil surface. Many rhizomes in one pot for fuller coverage.

care.harvest: Decorative — opens and closes with light

tips: The leaves open when bright and close in dim light or at night — a delightful daily rhythm.
Goes dormant in summer — die-back is normal. Rest dry for 4–6 weeks then restart.
Multiple bulbs in one pot creates a much fuller, more lush display.
Pink flowers appear above the purple leaves — very pretty combination.
warning: Toxic to cats and dogs if eaten in large quantities. Contains oxalic acid.

name: African Violet
tagline: The most popular flowering windowsill plant. Compact, beautiful, and can flower year-round.

water.detail: Water from below — pour water into the saucer and let the plant absorb what it needs. Never wet the leaves.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light — no direct sun which scorches leaves and flowers. North or east-facing window is ideal.

soil.value: African violet mix or peat-free compost
soil.detail: Specialist African violet compost or peat-free with added perlite.

temp.detail: Consistent warmth. Cold draughts and temperature fluctuations cause leaf damage.

seedDepth.detail: Insert a leaf stalk (petiole) into moist compost. New plantlets emerge from the base of the stalk in 4–8 weeks.

tips: Water from below only — wet leaves cause permanent brown spots.
Remove spent flowers and yellowing leaves regularly to encourage continuous blooming.
Feed every 2 weeks with a specialist African violet fertiliser.
East or west-facing windowsill is the sweet spot — bright but no direct midday sun.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets. Safe choice.

name: Lipstick Plant
tagline: Tubular red flowers that emerge from dark tubes like lipstick being applied. Trails beautifully.

water.detail: Water when top 2–3cm is dry. Reduce in winter during rest period.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light triggers the best flowering. Too little light = few or no flowers.

soil.detail: Mix of potting compost and orchid bark for excellent drainage.

temp.detail: No cold draughts or below 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: Root 8–10cm tip cuttings in moist compost.

tips: Needs a cooler, drier winter rest (4–6 weeks at around 15°C with reduced watering) to trigger spring flowering.
Hang in a basket where the trailing stems and dramatic flowers can be seen from below.
Trim back after flowering to encourage new growth and more flowers next season.
Mist occasionally — it appreciates some humidity.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets.

name: Goldfish Plant
tagline: Trailing stems with tiny paired leaves and vivid orange-red flowers that really do look like goldfish.

water.detail: Keep moist during flowering. Allow to dry slightly between waterings otherwise.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light. Direct sun scorches the small leaves.

soil.detail: Mix of peat-free compost and perlite. Good drainage essential.

temp.detail: No cold. Consistent warmth encourages the best flowering.

seedDepth.detail: Tip cuttings root easily in moist compost.

tips: A cool winter rest (15°C for 4–6 weeks) triggers the spectacular spring flower display.
Hang in a basket to appreciate the trailing stems covered in orange flowers.
Mist occasionally — appreciates humidity.
Feed monthly during the growing season with a high-potassium fertiliser.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets.

name: Passionflower
tagline: One of the most exotic-looking flowers you can grow at home. The intricate structure is unforgettable.

water.detail: Keep moist during growing season. Reduce significantly in winter.

sun.detail: Needs as much sun as possible to flower well. South-facing window is ideal.

soil.detail: Good quality compost with perlite. Feed regularly during the growing season.

temp.detail: More cold-hardy than most. Can overwinter in a cool frost-free place.

seedDepth.detail: Semi-ripe cuttings in summer root well. Seeds need warmth (25°C) and take 1–3 months.

care.harvest: Flowers summer–autumn. Fruit on mature plants.
care.spacing: Climber — needs a trellis or support

tips: Prune back hard in early spring — it flowers on new growth and pruning encourages vigorous flowering stems.
Give it a trellis or canes to climb — it grasps with tendrils and grows fast.
Orange fruits may appear after good summers — edible but not as flavourful as commercial passion fruit.
Feed weekly with a high-potassium fertiliser from May to September.
warning: The leaves and unripe fruit are mildly toxic. The ripe fruit is edible. Keep away from young children.

name: Purple Velvet Plant
tagline: Deep green leaves covered in vivid purple velvet hairs. Unlike anything else on a windowsill.

water.detail: Water when top soil is dry. Overwatering causes root rot.

sun.detail: Bright light brings out the vivid purple. Low light = mostly green leaves.

soil.detail: Standard compost with perlite.

temp.detail: No cold draughts or frost.

seedDepth.detail: Root tip cuttings in water or moist compost very easily.

tips: Never mist — the velvet hairs trap water and cause rot. Water from below only.
Pinch out flower buds — the orange flowers smell unpleasant and the plant deteriorates after flowering.
Pinch growing tips regularly for a bushy, full plant.
The younger the leaves, the more vivid the purple — older leaves turn more green.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets.

name: Haworthia
tagline: The ideal succulent for low-light conditions. Striped zebra-patterned leaves in a compact rosette.

water.detail: Let soil dry completely. Very drought-tolerant.

sun.detail: Unlike most succulents, tolerates lower light. Direct sun scorches the tips.

soil.detail: Fast-draining mix with grit or perlite.

temp.detail: Tolerates cool temperatures. Not frost-hardy.

seedDepth.detail: Separate pups that appear at the base and pot individually.

tips: One of the few succulents that survives in lower-light conditions — great for less sunny rooms.
The white raised markings on the leaves are the plant's natural texture, not a problem.
Very slow-growing — be patient. Don't repot unnecessarily.
Group multiple in a shallow dish for a striking display.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets. Safe choice.

name: Gasteria
tagline: Tongue-shaped mottled leaves in stacked rows. A tough, shade-tolerant succulent rarely seen in shops.

water.detail: Let soil dry completely. Very drought-tolerant.

sun.detail: One of the most shade-tolerant succulents. Direct sun causes reddish discolouration.

soil.detail: Fast-draining gritty compost.

temp.detail: Tolerates cool temperatures but not frost.

seedDepth.detail: Separate pups at repotting time.

care.harvest: Decorative — produces tubular orange-red flowers on long stalks

tips: Even better suited to low light than haworthia — a genuine shade-tolerant succulent.
Produces attractive orange-red tubular flowers in spring if kept in good light.
Very slow-growing — a good indicator that you're not overwatering.
Related to aloe — shares similar care needs and even similar soothing gel in the leaves.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets.

name: Kalanchoe
tagline: Clusters of tiny bright flowers for months. One of the most popular gift plants worldwide.

water.detail: Let soil dry between waterings. Succulent leaves store water — overwatering is the main mistake.

sun.detail: Bright light keeps it compact and triggers re-blooming.

soil.detail: Fast-draining compost. Never sit in water.

temp.detail: No cold or frost. Consistent room temperature is fine.

seedDepth.detail: Root easily from leaf or stem cuttings in dry compost.

tips: To trigger re-blooming: give 14 hours of complete darkness for 6 weeks in autumn, then return to bright light.
Deadhead spent flower clusters to encourage the next flush.
Feed monthly with a high-potassium fertiliser during the growing season.
Very easy to propagate — a single leaf on dry compost will root and produce a new plant.
warning: Toxic to cats and dogs. Can cause vomiting, diarrhoea, and cardiac issues.

name: Stonecrop Sedum
tagline: Trailing stems of chubby blue-green leaves. The donkey's tail succulent is one of the most charming trailers.

water.detail: Soak and let dry completely. Very drought-tolerant.

sun.detail: Needs bright light for the best colour and compact growth.

soil.detail: Fast-draining gritty compost.

temp.detail: Not frost-hardy. Tolerates cooler indoor temperatures.

seedDepth.detail: Leaves that fall off can be laid on dry compost to root. Stem sections also root easily.

tips: The leaves fall off extremely easily when touched — handle as little as possible.
Any fallen leaves that land on dry compost will often root on their own — free plants.
Hang in a basket where the trailing stems can grow long undisturbed.
Pink flowers may appear in summer at the tips of the stems.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets.

name: String of Buttons
tagline: Stacked triangular leaves on trailing stems create a geometric tower effect. Unique and easy.

water.detail: Drought-tolerant. Let dry completely between waterings.

sun.detail: Bright light is essential for the compact stacked leaf pattern and pinkish colouring.

soil.detail: Fast-draining gritty compost.

temp.detail: Tolerates cool temperatures.

seedDepth.detail: Cut a stem and let callous for a day. Push into dry cactus compost.

tips: The pink edges on the leaves intensify in bright sun — a sign of a very happy plant.
Trailing varieties look great in a hanging pot or cascading from a shelf.
Very forgiving — a good starter succulent.
Pairs beautifully with other succulents in a shallow dish arrangement.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets.

name: Earth Star Bromeliad
tagline: Flat, starfish-shaped rosettes with striped pink and green leaves. Unlike any other houseplant.

water.detail: Water into the rosette cup and keep the compost slightly moist.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light maintains the best colouring.

soil.detail: Fast-draining, low-nutrient mix.

temp.detail: Tropical plant — no cold draughts.

seedDepth.detail: Small pups appear at the base — pot these up when they have their own roots.

tips: Unlike other bromeliads, earth stars prefer to be watered into the compost as well as the central cup.
The striped colouring is most vivid in bright light.
Unlike most bromeliads, these are quite compact and stay small — perfect for terrariums.
Produces pups slowly after flowering — pot these up for new plants.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets.

name: Venus Flytrap
tagline: The most famous carnivorous plant. Snaps shut on insects in a fraction of a second.

water.value: Keep in a tray of rainwater
water.detail: Always use rainwater or distilled water — tap water minerals kill it. Keep the pot sitting in 2–3cm of water at all times.

sun.detail: Needs direct sunlight. A south-facing windowsill or outdoors in summer.

soil.detail: 50% sphagnum peat, 50% perlite or horticultural sand. Never regular compost — nutrients kill it.

temp.detail: Needs a winter dormancy of 3–4 months at near-freezing to survive long-term. This is not optional.

seedDepth.detail: Leaf pullings (pulling a leaf from the base) can produce new plants. Seeds are slow.

tips: Never feed it hamburger, cheese, or dead insects — the trap digests only freshly caught live prey or can be fed live mealworms.
Never trigger the traps for fun — it wastes enormous energy and weakens the plant.
The winter dormancy is non-negotiable for long-term health. A fridge works.
Only 3–4 traps open at once is normal — older traps turn black and die as new ones grow.
warning: Non-toxic but requires very specific care. Most plants die within a year due to wrong water, wrong soil, or no winter rest.

name: Pitcher Plant
tagline: Elegant tubular pitchers trap and digest insects. A genuinely alien-looking garden or windowsill plant.

water.detail: Must use rainwater or distilled water only. Sit pot in 2–3cm of water at all times during growing season.

sun.detail: Needs maximum direct sunlight for the best pitcher development and colouring.

soil.detail: 50% sphagnum peat, 50% perlite. Never regular compost.

temp.detail: Most sarracenia are cold-hardy and actually need freezing winters for long-term health.

seedDepth.detail: Divide the rhizome in spring. Seeds take 2–3 years to reach a decent size.

care.harvest: Pitchers trap insects — no manual feeding needed near windows

tips: No fertilising ever — the plant gets all its nutrition from insects. Nutrients kill it.
The pitchers fill with rainwater and digestive enzymes naturally.
In autumn, as pitchers die back, this is normal. New pitchers emerge each spring.
Cold-hardy varieties (most North American sarracenia) can be grown outdoors year-round in cool climates.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets. The digestive fluid in the pitchers is harmless.

name: Sundew
tagline: Glistening sticky tentacles trap insects and slowly curl around them. Beautiful and fascinating.

water.detail: Rainwater or distilled only. Sit in 1–2cm of water at all times during growing season.

sun.detail: Bright light keeps the tentacles vivid red. More sun = more colour and stickiness.

soil.detail: 50% sphagnum peat, 50% perlite. Never regular compost.

temp.detail: Cape sundew (D. capensis) is one of the most forgiving. Only a light rest needed.

seedDepth.detail: Lay a leaf on moist sphagnum — new plantlets emerge. Easy.

tips: One of the easiest carnivorous plants — more forgiving than Venus flytraps.
The sticky glands (tentacles) glisten in light — beautiful when backlit.
Catches fungus gnats reliably — a genuinely useful pest control plant.
Goes semi-dormant in winter — reduce watering slightly but never let it dry out.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets. The digestive secretions are harmless to humans.

name: Thanksgiving Cactus
tagline: Similar to Christmas cactus but flowers in November. Vivid tubular flowers in pink, red, orange, or white.

water.detail: More frequent than desert cacti. Keep moist during flowering, reduce after.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light. Direct sun bleaches flowers.

soil.detail: Potting compost with perlite.

temp.detail: Needs cool nights (below 15°C) in autumn to trigger flowering.

seedDepth.detail: Twist off 2–3 leaf segments, let callous, push into moist compost.

tips: Distinguished from Christmas cactus by its leaf segments: Thanksgiving cactus has pointed teeth, Christmas cactus has rounded scallops.
Cool nights in September–October trigger bud formation.
Feed monthly from spring to early autumn.
Can live for decades — gets larger and more spectacular with age.
warning: Mildly toxic to cats and dogs.

name: String of Turtles
tagline: Tiny round leaves with intricate white veining that really does look like turtle shells. A miniature wonder.

water.detail: Let soil dry between waterings. Very drought-tolerant like all peperomias.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light brings out the best markings. Too little light fades the pattern.

soil.detail: Chunky mix of potting compost and perlite.

temp.detail: No cold below 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: Lay stem sections on moist compost and pin down. Roots form at the nodes.

tips: Grows slowly — be patient and don't overwater while waiting.
Perfect in a hanging pot or at the edge of a shelf where the trailing stems can hang.
High humidity helps growth — but avoid misting directly on the leaves.
The intricate veining is most visible in bright light.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets.

name: Tineke Rubber Plant
tagline: The variegated version of the classic rubber plant — creamy white, green, and pink leaves.

water.detail: Water when top 3–4cm is dry. Overwatering causes root rot.

sun.detail: Needs more light than the plain green rubber plant to maintain the variegation.

soil.detail: Good quality compost with perlite.

temp.detail: No cold draughts.

seedDepth.detail: Allow the white sap to dry on the cut end before rooting in moist compost.

tips: The variegation fades in low light — brighter spot equals more white and pink in the leaves.
Wipe the large leaves with a damp cloth monthly.
New leaves emerge from a red sheath (cataphyll) which falls off naturally.
The white sap is a skin irritant — wear gloves when taking cuttings.
warning: Toxic to cats and dogs. The milky sap irritates skin and causes digestive issues if ingested.

name: Swiss Cheese Vine
tagline: The smaller, more delicate cousin of the iconic monstera. Holey leaves on trailing or climbing vines.

water.detail: Water when top 3cm is dry. Overwatering is the main problem.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light produces more holes in the leaves. Low light = smaller, less fenestrated leaves.

soil.detail: Chunky potting mix with perlite. Roots need air.

temp.detail: No cold below 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: Take a cutting with a node and root in water or moist compost.

tips: Grows faster and trails further than the large-leaf monstera.
Give it a pole to climb — produces bigger, more holey leaves when it can climb.
The holes in the leaves (fenestrations) are more pronounced in brighter light.
Very easy to propagate in water — the nodes root quickly.
warning: Toxic to cats, dogs, and humans if ingested.

name: Thai Constellation Monstera
tagline: The most coveted variegated houseplant. Creamy yellow speckled leaves on the iconic monstera. Expensive — but stunning.

water.detail: Water when top 3–5cm is dry. Overwatering damages the variegation.

sun.detail: Needs more light than the plain monstera to sustain the white/cream variegation. Low light fades the pattern.

soil.detail: Chunky potting mix with perlite and bark. Excellent aeration essential.

temp.detail: Consistent warmth. No cold draughts.

seedDepth.detail: Cuttings taken below a node. The variegation is genetic — all propagated plants maintain the pattern.

tips: The white/cream sections of the leaves have no chlorophyll — they cannot photosynthesise. The plant works harder with less green leaf area, so it grows more slowly than regular monstera.
Give it a moss pole — it grows bigger fenestrated leaves when it can climb.
The Thai Constellation is tissue-culture propagated — meaning the variegation is stable, unlike other variegated monsteras.
Feed monthly in spring and summer with a balanced fertiliser.
warning: Toxic to cats, dogs, and humans if ingested. Very expensive plant — insure against pet damage.

name: Marble Queen Pothos
tagline: White and green marbled leaves on the most popular trailing houseplant. Slightly fussier than golden pothos.

water.detail: Water when top 3cm is dry. Highly variegated leaves mean slower growth and slightly more care needed.

sun.detail: Needs more light than green pothos varieties to maintain the white marbling.

soil.detail: Standard potting compost with perlite.

temp.detail: No cold below 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: Root in water — quick and easy.

tips: The white sections have no chlorophyll — the plant grows more slowly than all-green varieties.
If new leaves come out all white, move to slightly lower light — the plant is losing too much chlorophyll.
Propagates easily in water — a great first propagation project.
The more white on the leaves, the more light it needs.
warning: Toxic to cats and dogs.

name: Neon Pothos
tagline: Electric lime-green leaves that glow in any room. The most vivid and eye-catching pothos variety.

water.detail: Water when top 3cm is dry.

sun.detail: Tolerates low light but the neon colour fades. Brighter light = more vivid electric green.

soil.detail: Standard compost with perlite.

temp.detail: No cold below 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: Root in water in 1–2 weeks.

tips: The neon colour is most intense in brighter light — in low light it fades to a more standard green.
Pairs dramatically with dark-leaved plants like ZZ or rubber plant.
Grows slightly faster than more variegated pothos varieties.
Looks spectacular trailing from a hanging pot where the lime-green really pops.
warning: Toxic to cats and dogs.

name: Scindapsus (Silver Pothos)
tagline: Velvety dark green leaves with metallic silver splashes. Often mistaken for pothos but richer in texture.

water.detail: Let top 3–4cm dry out. Very drought-tolerant.

sun.detail: Tolerates lower light than most trailing plants. The silver markings are most vivid in bright conditions.

soil.detail: Standard compost with perlite.

temp.detail: No cold below 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: Root in water or moist compost.

tips: The velvety texture is much more tactile than regular pothos — worth touching.
'Argyraeus' variety has small silver spots. 'Exotica' has large silver patches — even more dramatic.
Very easy to grow and propagate — a slightly more unusual alternative to standard pothos.
The leaves are heavier than pothos so it trails with a satisfying weight.
warning: Toxic to cats and dogs.

name: Bird's Nest Snake Plant
tagline: The compact, rosette form of the snake plant. Perfect for small spaces, shelves, and desks.

water.detail: Extremely drought-tolerant. Let soil dry completely.

sun.detail: Tolerates deep shade to full sun. Virtually impossible to provide wrong light.

soil.detail: Cactus mix or standard compost with grit.

temp.detail: Wide range. No cold below 10°C.

seedDepth.detail: Divide the rosette or take leaf sections.

tips: Perfect for desks, bookshelves, and bathroom windowsills.
Stays compact — never outgrows its space like the tall variety.
The same near-indestructible care as standard snake plant.
Groups of three in different pots make a striking arrangement.
warning: Mildly toxic to cats and dogs.
